title: A1C (hemoglobin A1C)
text: A1C is a blood test that reflects your average blood sugar over about three months. It is used to monitor diabetes care. Your clinician sets a personal target.

title: Type 2 diabetes
text: Type 2 diabetes means the body has trouble using insulin well. Treatment often includes nutrition, activity, medications, and follow-up tests like A1C.

title: High blood pressure (hypertension)
text: Blood pressure is the force of blood against artery walls. High readings over time can raise risk of heart attack and stroke. Home monitoring and medications are common tools.

title: LDL cholesterol
text: LDL is often called 'bad' cholesterol because higher levels are linked to plaque buildup in arteries. Diet, exercise, and medicines can all play a role.

title: HDL cholesterol
text: HDL is sometimes called 'good' cholesterol. Higher HDL is generally associated with lower cardiovascular risk, but targets depend on the whole picture.

title: Creatinine and kidney function
text: Creatinine is a waste product muscles make. Blood levels are used with other tests to estimate kidney function. Results should be interpreted by a clinician.

title: Troponin
text: Troponin is a protein released when heart muscle is injured. It is commonly measured when doctors evaluate possible heart attack.

title: BNP test
text: BNP is a hormone marker sometimes used when evaluating shortness of breath or heart failure symptoms. It is one clue among many.

title: Hospital admission
text: An admission note explains why you were hospitalized, early findings, and the initial plan. It is not the same as the full discharge instructions.

title: Discharge summary
text: A discharge summary reviews your hospital stay, diagnoses, procedures, medicines to continue, and follow-up plans.

title: Electrocardiogram (ECG/EKG)
text: An ECG records the heart's electrical activity. It can help find rhythm problems or signs of reduced blood flow to the heart muscle.

title: Heart attack (STEMI)
text: STEMI is a severe heart attack pattern seen on ECG. It usually needs urgent treatment to restore blood flow to a blocked artery.

title: Aspirin
text: Aspirin can reduce blood clotting and is used for some heart conditions. It can also cause stomach bleeding. Do not change aspirin use without clinician advice.

title: Metformin
text: Metformin is a common pill for type 2 diabetes. It mainly lowers liver glucose production and improves insulin sensitivity. Side effects can include stomach upset.

title: Insulin basics
text: Insulin helps sugar move from blood into cells. Dosing is individualized and must be directed by a clinician.

title: Hospice care
text: Hospice focuses on comfort and quality of life for people with a serious illness nearing the end of life. Teams include nurses, doctors, social workers, and chaplains.

title: Palliative care
text: Palliative care helps symptoms and stress of serious illness at any stage. It can be provided alongside treatments meant to cure or control disease.

title: Oxygen saturation (SpO2)
text: SpO2 estimates how much oxygen is in your blood using a finger sensor. Low readings can mean lung or heart problems and should be interpreted in context.

title: Complete blood count (CBC)
text: A CBC measures blood cells like red cells, white cells, and platelets. It helps evaluate infection, anemia, and other conditions.

title: Potassium (K)
text: Potassium is an electrolyte important for nerves and muscles, including the heart. Very high or very low levels can be dangerous.

title: Sodium (Na)
text: Sodium is an electrolyte tied to fluid balance. Abnormal levels can relate to hydration, kidneys, or hormones.

title: Liver enzymes (ALT/AST)
text: ALT and AST are enzymes that can rise with liver irritation or injury. Many causes exist, from medications to infections.

title: Blood thinners and INR
text: INR is used to monitor warfarin therapy. The goal range is individualized. Food and other medicines can affect results.

title: Mood symptoms
text: Feeling sad or hopeless can be medical conditions. If you have thoughts of self-harm, seek emergency help immediately.

title: Vaccines
text: Vaccines train the immune system against specific infections. Schedules depend on age, health conditions, and public health guidance.

title: Drug allergies
text: A drug allergy is an immune reaction to a medicine. It is different from side effects. Always tell clinicians about known allergies.

title: Pain scales
text: Clinicians often ask you to rate pain from 0 to 10. This helps track changes over time; it does not replace a full exam.

title: Physical therapy
text: Physical therapy uses exercise, education, and hands-on techniques to improve movement, strength, and function after injury or surgery.

title: Sleep apnea
text: Sleep apnea means breathing pauses during sleep. It can cause daytime sleepiness and strain the heart. Diagnosis often involves a sleep study.

title: Stroke warning signs
text: Sudden face droop, arm weakness, or speech trouble can be stroke signs and need emergency care. Time matters for treatment options.

title: Kidney stones
text: Kidney stones are hard crystals that can cause severe flank pain and blood in urine. Treatment depends on size and location.

title: Urinary tract infection (UTI)
text: UTIs can cause burning with urination, urgency, or fever. Treatment is usually antibiotics prescribed by a clinician.

title: COPD
text: COPD is a lung disease that makes airflow feel blocked. Symptoms include cough and shortness of breath. Smoking cessation is a key part of care.

title: Asthma
text: Asthma causes airway narrowing and wheezing. Rescue inhalers treat sudden symptoms; controller medicines reduce flare frequency.

title: TSH (thyroid)
text: TSH is a pituitary hormone used as a screen for thyroid gland activity. Results are interpreted with symptoms and sometimes other thyroid tests.

title: Vitamin D
text: Vitamin D supports bones and other body functions. Levels are checked with a blood test; supplementation should follow clinician guidance.

title: MRI scan
text: MRI uses magnets and radio waves to create detailed images. It is useful for brain, spine, and joint problems. Metal implants can be a safety issue.

title: CT scan
text: CT combines X-rays to make cross-sectional images. It involves radiation exposure; benefits and risks are weighed by the ordering clinician.

title: Colonoscopy
text: Colonoscopy uses a camera to examine the colon. It can screen for cancer and evaluate bleeding or inflammation.

title: Mammogram
text: A mammogram is an X-ray picture of breast tissue used to screen for cancer or evaluate lumps.

title: Anticoagulants
text: Anticoagulants reduce clot formation. They increase bleeding risk and require careful monitoring and medication review.

title: Statins
text: Statins lower LDL cholesterol and reduce cardiovascular risk for many people. Muscle aches can occur; report new symptoms to a clinician.

title: Anemia
text: Anemia means low red blood cells or hemoglobin. It can cause fatigue and shortness of breath. Causes range from iron deficiency to chronic disease.

title: Blood glucose
text: Blood glucose reflects sugar in the blood at a moment in time. Fasting and after-meal patterns mean different things.

title: A1C goals
text: A1C targets are personalized. Older adults or people with hypoglycemia risk may have less strict goals than younger adults.

title: Diabetes foot care
text: Diabetes can reduce foot sensation and healing. Daily foot checks and prompt care for cuts help prevent serious infections.

title: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)
text: DKA is a serious condition with high blood sugar and ketones. It can include nausea, vomiting, and rapid breathing and needs emergency care.

title: Low blood sugar
text: Low blood sugar can cause shakiness, sweating, confusion, or fainting. People on insulin or secretagogues should have a plan from their clinician.

title: Flu vaccine
text: Seasonal flu vaccines reduce influenza risk and complications. Annual vaccination is recommended for many age groups.

title: COVID-19 vaccines
text: COVID-19 vaccines reduce severe disease risk. Guidance updates over time; follow local public health recommendations.

title: Acid reflux (GERD)
text: GERD causes heartburn or regurgitation when stomach acid flows back into the esophagus. Lifestyle changes and medicines can help.

title: Inflammatory bowel disease
text: IBD includes conditions like Crohn disease and ulcerative colitis. Flares can cause diarrhea, pain, and weight loss and need specialist care.

title: Cellulitis
text: Cellulitis is a skin infection that causes spreading redness, warmth, and swelling. It often needs antibiotics.

title: Concussion
text: A concussion is a mild traumatic brain injury that can cause headache, dizziness, and trouble concentrating. Rest and gradual return to activity are common.

title: Deep vein thrombosis (DVT)
text: DVT is a clot in a deep vein, often in the leg. It can cause swelling and pain and may lead to lung clots if untreated.

title: Pulmonary embolism (PE)
text: PE is a clot in lung arteries. Symptoms can include sudden shortness of breath and chest pain. This is an emergency.

title: Heart failure
text: Heart failure means the heart struggles to meet the body's needs. Symptoms include swelling, shortness of breath, and reduced exercise tolerance.

title: Atrial fibrillation
text: Atrial fibrillation is an irregular heart rhythm that can increase stroke risk. Treatment may include rate control, rhythm control, and stroke prevention.

title: Chronic kidney disease (CKD)
text: CKD means lasting kidney damage or reduced function. It is staged by lab tests and managed to slow progression and protect the heart.

title: Kidney failure (ESRD)
text: End-stage kidney disease may require dialysis or transplant. Decisions are individualized with a nephrology team.

title: Organ transplant
text: Transplant replaces a failing organ with a donor organ. Lifelong medications help prevent rejection and need close follow-up.

title: Antibiotics
text: Antibiotics treat bacterial infections, not viruses. Finishing the prescribed course depends on the infection; ask your clinician if unsure.

title: Opioid pain medicines
text: Opioids can reduce severe pain but carry risks like sedation, constipation, dependence, and overdose. Use only as directed by a clinician.

title: NSAIDs (ibuprofen/naproxen)
text: NSAIDs reduce pain and inflammation but can affect kidneys, blood pressure, and stomach lining. Long-term use needs clinician oversight.

title: Steroid medicines (prednisone)
text: Steroids reduce inflammation but can raise blood sugar, blood pressure, and infection risk. Tapering must be clinician-directed.

title: Radiation therapy
text: Radiation treats some cancers by targeting tumor cells. Side effects depend on the body area treated.

title: Chemotherapy
text: Chemotherapy uses medicines to treat cancer. Side effects vary widely by regimen and person.

title: Advance care planning
text: Advance directives describe wishes if you cannot speak for yourself. Examples include a health care proxy and living will.

title: DNR/DNI orders
text: Do-not-resuscitate (DNR) and do-not-intubate (DNI) orders describe preferences for emergency interventions. They are decided with clinicians and family.

title: CPR
text: CPR is emergency chest compressions and rescue breathing for cardiac arrest. Outcomes depend on cause, timing, and overall health.
